stem: In a certain graph, the six vertices have degrees \( [[a]], [[b]], [[c]], [[d]], [[e]], \) and \( [[f]] \). How many edges does the graph have?
solution: By the handshake lemma, the sum of the vertex degrees equals twice the number of edges. The degree sum is \( [[a]] + [[b]] + [[c]] + [[d]] + [[e]] + [[f]] \), so the number of edges is half of this: \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the sum of the degrees itself; by the handshake lemma the edge count is only half of that sum.
This is off by one from the correct edge count.
This is off by one from the correct edge count.
This is off by two from the correct edge count.
This is off by two from the correct edge count.
This is off by three from the correct edge count.

stem: A graph has \( [[n]] \) vertices, and every vertex has degree \( [[r]] \) (the graph is \( [[r]] \)-regular). How many edges does the graph have?
solution: By the handshake lemma, the sum of the degrees is \( [[n]] \cdot [[r]] \) (since all \( [[n]] \) vertices have degree \( [[r]] \)), and this equals twice the number of edges. So the edge count is \( \frac{[[n]] \cdot [[r]]}{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the degree sum itself; by the handshake lemma the edge count is only half of that sum.
This adds the vertex count and the common degree instead of using the handshake lemma.
This is the maximum possible number of edges on \( [[n]] \) vertices, ignoring the actual degree.
This is the degree sum plus an extra stray unit.
This is off by one from the correct edge count.
This is off by one from the correct edge count.

stem: A graph is \( [[r]] \)-regular (every vertex has degree \( [[r]] \)) and has \( [[E]] \) edges in total. How many vertices does the graph have?
solution: By the handshake lemma, the degree sum equals twice the edge count: \( n \cdot [[r]] = 2 \cdot [[E]] \). Solving for \( n \) gives \( n = \dfrac{2 \cdot [[E]]}{[[r]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies the edge count by the degree instead of solving the handshake-lemma equation for \( n \).
This adds the edge count and the degree instead of solving for \( n \).
This subtracts the degree from the edge count instead of solving for \( n \).
This forgets to multiply the edge count by 2 before dividing by the degree.
This is off by one from the correct number of vertices.
This is off by one from the correct number of vertices.

stem: In a graph, \( [[p]] \) of the vertices have degree \( [[a]] \) and the remaining \( [[q]] \) vertices have degree \( [[b]] \). How many edges does the graph have?
solution: By the handshake lemma, the total degree sum is \( [[p]] \cdot [[a]] + [[q]] \cdot [[b]] \), and this equals twice the number of edges. So the edge count is \( \dfrac{[[p]] \cdot [[a]] + [[q]] \cdot [[b]]}{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the degree sum itself; by the handshake lemma the edge count is only half of that sum.
This adds the two vertex counts instead of using their degrees at all.
This is the maximum possible number of edges on \( [[p]]+[[q]] \) vertices, ignoring the actual degrees.
This only halves the contribution from the first group, forgetting to halve the second.
This is off by one from the correct edge count.
This is off by one from the correct edge count.
